metadata.title: Doxil's Real Job Isn't Hitting the Tumor — It's Protecting the Heart
metadata.purpose: vox-explainer slate cut of Cancer Nanomedicine card 12. Source chapter: cancer-nanomedicine/chapters/03-nanocarrier-platforms-liposomes-polymeric-and-albumin-particles.md (Doxil cardiac protection mechanism only).
metadata.clock: narration (ElevenLabs) — durations below are word-count estimates until GATE 0 audio lock

style_bible.visual_style: editorial paper collage on real newsprint scan; desaturated plates; flat annotation plane; serif labels with hairline underlines; hand-drawn annotation ring used once per graphic at most
style_bible.color_palette: cream ground, charcoal serif type, teal = protected/spared/good, crimson = damaged/exposed/broken, gold highlighter as the single editor's-pen voice

metadata.color_semantics: teal #1F6F5C = protected / spared / cardiac-safe; crimson #BF3339 = exposed / damaged / toxic (free drug, heart exposure). Never swap mid-film.
metadata.note: Card exclusions honored: NO Onivyde, NO Vyxeos, NO three-benefit framework, NO EPR mechanism detail, NO platform decision tree. One correction: benefit is sparing the heart, not loading the tumor. The Question gap formula appears ON SCREEN in B03 and in narration.
_variant_todo: rewrite every beat narration_text in the Wonder register (voices/wonder/VOICE.md + MEDHAVY.md) — voice only, facts unchanged
signature tangent 0-1 per video, ONLY on a clear opportunity (see SKILL)
swap the outro to the MEDHAVY outro from AUTHOR.MD :: Medhavy.com
then build audience-namespaced (audio in the new voice, scenes in the new palette)

beats.narration_text: The most famous cancer nanoparticle in history. Celebrated for decades. And its best-documented benefit isn't what most people in the field think it is — including people who have used it.

card.copy: Doxil's Real Job Isn't Hitting the Tumor —

beats.narration_text: A patient with ovarian cancer receives standard chemotherapy: doxorubicin. Potent. Effective against the tumor. But doctors are watching the cumulative dose carefully — because this drug is quietly accumulating in the heart muscle with every cycle.

props.headline: A patient with ovarian cancer. Standard chemotherapy: doxorubicin. Potent. Effective against…

beats.scene_description: An oncologist in consultation, reviewing a patient chart with a dosage schedule; clinical setting; slow push toward the chart; desaturated editorial treatment on newsprint ground
beats.image_prompt: B02, oncologist in a clinical examination room reviewing a printed patient dosage schedule for chemotherapy, close-up on the chart showing cumulative dose numbers, aged newsprint editorial treatment, desaturated collage, flat light, no faces identifiable

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [STILL·ai] oncologist reviews cumulative dox dose

beats.narration_text: Here is the puzzle. Doxil was approved because it delivers doxorubicin to tumors. But the trial outcome that actually got it approved was cardiac protection. How did engineering a tumor-targeting nanoparticle end up protecting a completely different organ?

card.copy: Doxil was approved because it delivers doxorubicin to tumors. But the trial outcome that got it approved was cardiac protection.
card.dek: How did fixing the tumor deliver a different organ?

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] THE QUESTION — on screen

beats.narration_text: Doxorubicin is one of the most effective chemotherapy drugs ever made. It attacks fast-dividing cells — tumors, yes. But cardiac muscle cells never stop working. They are exposed to every dose the blood carries, cycle after cycle.

production_viz.label: free dox distributes everywhere
production_viz.mechanic: a central vessel shape; particles fan out left to tumor (teal) and right to heart (crimson) — both sides receive drug; crimson heart label prominent

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] free dox fans to both tumor and heart

beats.narration_text: The damage is cumulative. Every dose adds to a running total of cardiac injury. Cross the threshold — around 360 milligrams per square meter over a lifetime — and cardiomyopathy risk rises sharply. The real cap on this drug's usefulness isn't tumor biology. It's the heart.

production_viz.label: cumulative cardiac dose threshold
production_viz.mechanic: a vertical bar fills crimson from bottom upward; a threshold line labeled '360 mg/m2 lifetime' near the top; bar stops just at the threshold with label 'cardiomyopathy risk'
production_viz.note: 360 mg/m2 is the established threshold — from chapter, verbatim

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] cumulative dose bar rises to cardiac threshold

beats.narration_text: Here is what Doxil actually does. It seals the drug inside a PEGylated liposome — a lipid bubble coated with polyethylene glycol chains that disguise it from the immune system. The drug stays locked inside while the particle is in circulation.

props.headline: Doxil's answer: seal the drug inside a PEGylated liposome. A lipid…

beats.scene_description: Stylized cross-section diagram of a PEGylated liposome — a hollow lipid-bilayer sphere with PEG chains on the outside and drug molecules shown inside the aqueous core; editorial desaturated illustration on newsprint
beats.image_prompt: B06, scientific diagram cross-section of a PEGylated liposome nanoparticle showing lipid bilayer membrane with polyethylene glycol chains on exterior and doxorubicin drug molecules in aqueous interior core, editorial flat illustration style, desaturated newsprint ground, no text labels in image

beats.narration_text: The sealed particle circulates through the body. It passes through cardiac microvasculature. The heart has normal, tight blood vessels — the drug stays inside the particle, unreleased. The heart sees only a fraction of what free doxorubicin would have delivered directly.

production_viz.label: particle passes heart — drug stays inside
production_viz.mechanic: a particle (small circle) moves through a vessel path toward a heart icon; heart stays teal (protected); particle continues through without releasing — contrast shown vs free drug trail (crimson dots) that would have reached the heart
production_viz.note: the key mechanism — sealed in particle, cardiac tissue sees far less

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] particle passes heart — teal/protected; free drug would have been crimson

beats.narration_text: Cardiac tissue drug levels fall dramatically — and this is Doxil's best-documented clinical result. The cap that governed doxorubicin's usefulness was never primarily about what happened at the tumor. It was about the heart. And now the heart is spared.

document.quote: The primary benefit is that less drug reaches the heart.
document.attribution: — the clinical mechanism behind Doxil's approval

beats.scene_description: Quote card with gold highlighter sweep on the key phrase 'less drug reaches the heart'

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [QUOTE] less drug reaches the heart

beats.narration_text: Here's the distinction that gets lost. Teams learn about EPR — the way nanoparticles accumulate in tumors through abnormally porous vessels. They reach for a PEGylated liposome to load the tumor. But Doxil's win was never primarily about the tumor. It was about what the particle kept away from the heart.

production_viz.label: the common misread — EPR vs cardiac protection
production_viz.mechanic: two column headers: 'What teams think' (crimson, EPR arrow into tumor) vs 'What Doxil actually does' (teal, drug blocked from heart); a crimson X over the EPR column; a teal check over the cardiac column
production_viz.note: the correction beat — EPR excluded per card, but the misread IS the lesson

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] misread vs actual — EPR claim vs cardiac protection

beats.narration_text: Confusing these two leads directly to bad decisions. A team with a drug that has no cardiac toxicity — but a solvent problem — copied Doxil's approach. Built a PEGylated liposome. Spent a year. Nothing. Because the problem they had wasn't the problem Doxil was designed to solve.

production_viz.label: wrong tool — mismatch between problem and solution
production_viz.mechanic: two boxes: left 'Their drug' (no cardiac toxicity, solvent problem — teal label); right 'Doxil solution' (protects heart — teal label). A large crimson X drawn between them: mismatch. Below: a wasted year marker
production_viz.note: the opening-case team's error from the chapter intro

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] wrong tool for wrong problem — mismatch

beats.narration_text: Here's an illustrative case. Patient A: six cycles of free doxorubicin at 60 milligrams per square meter. Cumulative cardiac dose: 360 milligrams per square meter — at the threshold. Patient B: same dose, same schedule, but Doxil. Same administered drug. Same tumor response rate. Cardiac tissue drug levels roughly 40 percent lower. Same administered dose. Very different exposure at the heart. That's the clinical win.

production_viz.label: illustrative: same dose, different heart exposure
production_viz.mechanic: two side-by-side columns: Patient A (Free Dox) and Patient B (Doxil). Each column shows 6 cycles stacking up as crimson (Patient A) and teal (Patient B) blocks. Patient A's cardiac bar reaches '360 mg/m2' threshold line in crimson. Patient B's cardiac bar shows '~40% lower' and stays teal well below threshold. Bracket: 'same tumor response rate'. Note: 'illustrative'
production_viz.note: ALL numbers are illustrative — labeled as such. 360 mg/m2 threshold from chapter. 40% lower cardiac exposure is illustrative per card.

beats.new_visual_element: [GRAPHIC] illustrative example: Patient A vs Patient B

beats.narration_text: Back to the question: how did fixing the tumor deliver a different organ? The answer is that it didn't fix the tumor — it sealed the drug away from the heart. The clinical benefit was cardiac protection all along. The EPR story was context. The cardiac story was the actual win.

card.copy: Doxil's win: less drug to the heart — not more to the tumor.

beats.new_visual_element: [CARD] endcard — cardiac protection was the win

build.suggested: suggested prompt: [CARD] endcard — cardiac protection was the win
